keeper.secret: The Keeper unfolds a page torn from a hidden ledger, ink blurred by salt and fear. It names a chamber beneath the lens where each keeper carved a confession before vanishing. Your own name is already scratched there in a hand that looks disturbingly like yours.

elder.memory: The Elder's gaze drifts past you to the tower light, as if listening to a voice from long ago. They recall a winter when the beam burned black for one minute, and every bell on the island rang without hands to pull them. Since then, they say, no one here fully trusts dawn.
elder.revelation: At last the Elder presses a rusted key into your palm and tells you it opens a door below the mechanism room. Behind it rests a chart no mapmaker would admit exists: routes the island takes when it drifts between storms. The final margin note reads, 'Do not let the light go dark while the tide is speaking.'

wanderer.past: The Wanderer laughs once, bitterly, and admits they were never stranded here at all. They came chasing the same underwater signal now haunting your dreams, and every year since has been a failed escape. They warn that the lighthouse doesn't just keep things out — it keeps certain people in.

archivist.hidden_lore: The Archivist unlocks a drawer hidden behind worm-eaten folios and sets out a brittle vellum map. It marks tunnels beneath the lighthouse that do not exist on any survey, each annotated with resonance values and dates spanning centuries. At the bottom, a note in crimson ink reads: 'When trust aligns with the beam, the sealed history answers back.'

distant_light.desc: A distant light blinks three times across the water — deliberate, rhythmic. Someone, or something, is out there.
distant_light.investigate: You follow the signal to the cliff's edge. The light vanishes before you arrive, but you find salt-worn marks in the stone — an old message system.

strange_sound.desc: A sound rises from the rocks below: not wind, not wave. Something between a moan and a word.
strange_sound.investigate: You press your ear to the cold stone. The sound resolves into syllables you cannot quite parse — but you recognise the cadence. It matches the keeper's old journals.

shadow_movement.desc: Something moves at the edge of your lantern light. Too large for an animal. Too fluid for a person.
shadow_movement.investigate: You raise the lantern and step forward. The shadow retreats, leaving a depression in the wet grass — the shape of a body, still warm.

tide_signal.desc: The tide is withdrawing in an unusual pattern, exposing rocks that should be submerged. The water moves against the current.
tide_signal.investigate: You watch the pattern for several minutes. The rocks form a sequence — the same sequence carved above the lighthouse door. The sea is sending a message.

lantern_flicker.desc: Your lantern stutters without wind. Three short pulses, one long. Then steady again.
lantern_flicker.investigate: You count the pattern carefully: three short, one long. You have seen this before — in the signal log the keeper kept by the lens housing. Someone knows you are here.

voice_in_wind.desc: The wind carries a fragment of speech — a name. Your name. Spoken once, clearly, from no direction at all.
voice_in_wind.investigate: You stand perfectly still and listen. The voice does not return. But the silence that follows is different — attentive, waiting. Something on the island has noticed you.

history_01.title: A Trading Post, Then Something Else
history_01.body: The island's first recorded inhabitants arrived as merchants, drawn by a natural harbour sheltered from the southern winds. Within five years, no cargo had left. The settlement turned inward, and the records from that period describe construction — always construction — with no explanation of what was being built or why.

history_02.title: The First Keeper's Redacted Name
history_02.body: In every official record, the name of the first lighthouse keeper has been struck through. Not removed — the ink is still visible beneath the redaction. What remains legible is a title: 'Warden of the Threshold,' which does not appear in any official registry of maritime posts.

history_03.title: What the Settlers Agreed To
history_03.body: The founding charter is forty-seven pages. Thirty-one of them describe ordinary matters: land division, trade rights, the harbour's governance. The final sixteen pages are written in a different hand, on paper of a different weight, and concern obligations the settlers agreed to without, it appears, being told who they were agreeing with.

history_04.title: Forty Years Without Record
history_04.body: Between the founding and the first surviving dated document lies a gap of approximately forty years. The archive acknowledges this. There is a single index card in Maren's handwriting that reads: 'See Gap Years file,' and a second card behind it that reads: 'Gap Years file: missing.'

history_05.title: The Feeding Schedule
history_05.body: A loose page, tucked between cottage floorboards: 'Third tide of the full moon — three measures of rendered seal fat, two of the compound, one measure of whatever the mechanism has produced that cycle.' The word 'feeding' appears in the margin twice. The author refers to the recipient only as 'it.'

history_06.title: Why One Year, Exactly
history_06.body: Keepers were required by charter to serve exactly one year — three hundred and sixty-five days, counted from the first lighting of the beam. Historians have attributed this to tradition, but the surviving keeper contracts specify a penalty for early departure that has no monetary value. The penalty is described as 'the forfeiture of what was pledged at threshold.'

history_07.title: Provisions for One, Perhaps Two
history_07.body: The annual supply ledger lists provisions sufficient for two people throughout the keeper years, despite all official records naming a single occupant. The annotation for this anomaly, written by an early harbourmaster, is four words: 'The keeper has company.' The ledger then skips a year.

history_08.title: Not Originally a Chapel
history_08.body: St. Maret's was built on foundations that predate the charter by at least two centuries. The original building had no seating, no altar, and no windows — only a single door and a central pit, now covered with flagstone. The chapel function was retrofitted. The original purpose has no name in any record.

history_09.title: That Isn't St. Maret
history_09.body: The figure in the stained glass window bears no resemblance to any canonised saint. The name 'Maret' appears in no ecclesiastical register. The figure's hands are positioned in a gesture documented in occult taxonomy as 'the binding welcome' — arms outstretched, palms turned inward, as if holding something that could otherwise escape.

history_10.title: What the Village Has Always Known
history_10.body: There is an unwritten agreement among the island's families, transmitted not in documents but in behaviour: do not ask what the lighthouse contains, do not disturb the keeper's schedule, do not speak of those who do not return. The children learn this before they learn to read. No one appears to have decided this. It simply is.

occult_01.title: Marks on the Threshold Stones
occult_01.body: The runes that appear on doorstep stones across the island are not decorative. Each is a fragment of a binding notation — incomplete alone, forming a circuit only when read in the sequence defined by the original surveyor's route through the island. The circuit has never been mapped in full. Maren believes it is deliberately fragmented.

occult_02.title: What the Vael Consumes
occult_02.body: The entity does not eat in any biological sense. What it draws from the keeper — and from the mechanism — is better described as temporal coherence: the stable relationship between past action and present state. Each loop resets this. Each loop costs something. The keeper ages in a way that does not correspond to calendar time.

occult_03.title: Ritual, Not Machinery
occult_03.body: The mechanism has moving parts. It has gears and shafts and a tolerance for physical maintenance. These are concessions to the keepers' need to feel useful. The actual function is ritual: a structured repetition of action that renews, each night, a compact made before the mechanism was built. The gears are secondary. The intention is primary.

occult_04.title: Who Benefits from the Loop
occult_04.body: The loop is not a malfunction. It was designed as a failsafe. If the keeper fails to renew the seal, the loop resets to a point before the failure, offering another attempt. This is, on balance, beneficial to the entity — it can never be permanently freed by a single mistake, but it also cannot be permanently bound by a single act of will. The loop protects both.

occult_05.title: A Warning in a Dead Language
occult_05.body: The cliff face above the northern cove bears markings that appear, in the right light, to be deliberate. They are. Translated from the Vael notation system, the message is a single imperative repeated in three registers: 'Do not call it by name.' There is no accompanying explanation of what happens if the instruction is not followed.

occult_06.title: The Shape Below the Cliff
occult_06.body: It is not a creature. It is not, properly speaking, a shape. The impression of a shape is produced by the way the water displaces around something that has no fixed physical form. It moves when the lighthouse beam passes over it. It stills when the beam fails. What it wants is a matter of considerable scholarly disagreement, which is to say: no one who has studied it closely has published afterwards.

occult_07.title: Three Kinds of Bound Spirit
occult_07.body: The taxonomy distinguishes bound spirits by their relationship to consent. First-tier entities were bound against their will — they resist and degrade. Second-tier entities were bound by agreement — they fulfil the compact but do not extend it. Third-tier entities cooperated in their own binding. The Vael does not appear cleanly in any category. This is the most disturbing finding in the file.

occult_08.title: Text That Appears at Low Tide
occult_08.body: Sections of the cave wall are legible only when the tide is fully out — perhaps two hours in every cycle. The writing is carved beneath the waterline and traced in a bioluminescent compound that the saltwater dims. Current translations account for approximately a third of the visible text. The legible sections are navigational in form but describe a route that does not correspond to any physical geography.

occult_09.title: How a Seal Breaks
occult_09.body: A seal breaks under three conditions: the ritual is performed incorrectly, the ritual is not performed at all, or the will of the bound entity overcomes the initial binding — which requires the entity to have accumulated sufficient temporal mass. The Vael's imprisonment has lasted long enough that the third condition is approaching. The first two remain the proximate risk.

occult_10.title: Outside the Loop's Boundary
occult_10.body: The loop has an edge. Beyond it, time runs normally — or so the notation implies. What exists at the boundary is uncertain. Some keeper accounts describe a feeling at the cliff's farthest point of stepping into a different kind of silence. One keeper wrote only: 'The air outside the island does not know us.' The loop's boundary may be coextensive with the binding radius.

maritime_01.title: Ships That Were Not Looking
maritime_01.body: In the collected harbour logs, no arriving vessel records having plotted a course to this island. They arrive having been bound elsewhere — for different ports, different coastlines — and find themselves here without understanding the navigation that brought them. Some turned back immediately. The ones that stayed long enough to ask questions rarely left.

maritime_02.title: The Log's Impossible Arrivals
maritime_02.body: Silas's predecessor kept meticulous records of arrival times, cross-referenced against tidal tables and wind speeds. At least eleven entries describe vessels arriving under conditions that, by the physics of sail and tide, should have taken four more hours. The predecessor noted this without explanation. Silas's own hand appears in the margins of these entries, but only to confirm them.

maritime_03.title: What the Wreck Was Carrying
maritime_03.body: The ship at the base of the north cliff is too old to have been recorded in the harbour log. Its cargo hold, accessed by divers in the previous century, contained sealed iron canisters — nine of them. Seven were empty. One contained a liquid with properties the examining scholar declined to describe in publishable detail. One was never opened. It remains on the seabed.

maritime_04.title: A Tide No Chart Can Explain
maritime_04.body: The island's tidal pattern does not correspond to the lunar tables that govern every surrounding body of water within two hundred miles. It corresponds, instead, to a cycle of approximately twenty-seven and a half days — close to the lunar month but consistently offset by six hours. The offset is not caused by the island's position. Something else produces it.

maritime_05.title: What the Beam Originally Signalled
maritime_05.body: The lighthouse beam, in its current configuration, rotates at a rate that produces a pulse pattern. Maritime navigation does not require a pulse — a steady light suffices. The pattern, read as notation, is a seven-syllable sequence in the Vael symbolic system. It broadcasts, every night, the same word. The word translates, approximately, as 'still.'

maritime_06.title: The Tidal Caves Are Not Natural
maritime_06.body: The cave system below the northern cliff has walls too regular to be erosion. The chambers are proportioned deliberately — their dimensions produce specific acoustic properties when the tide moves through them. Whether this was designed for communication or for containment is unclear. The engineering required would have been considerable. No record of its construction survives.

maritime_07.title: Something Shaped the Seabed
maritime_07.body: Sounding surveys of the surrounding water reveal a basin formation below the lighthouse that does not correspond to any geological process. The basin is circular, approximately three hundred metres across, and its walls are vertical. At its centre, the sounding line goes slack without finding bottom. Whatever is below that point does not return an echo.

maritime_08.title: Why Sailors Used This Light
maritime_08.body: For two centuries, certain experienced navigators sought this lighthouse's beam as a reference point. Not because it was bright — several nearer lights were brighter — but because their accounts describe it as uniquely stable. 'It does not flicker in the fog,' one log records. 'It maintains its rhythm when all other lights fail.' They did not know why. They trusted it regardless.

maritime_09.title: Ships That Found Us and Stayed
maritime_09.body: Seventeen vessels arrive in the surviving records and have no subsequent entry in any port register, anywhere. They docked, offloaded, and then nothing. Some of the crew names appear in the island census from following decades under different circumstances than their ship manifests would suggest. The vessels themselves were, in several cases, resailed under new registry with different names.

maritime_10.title: Objects That Do Not Belong Here
maritime_10.body: Items recovered from the seabed near the island include ceramics of no known regional style, a bronze object whose function is unidentified, and — most troublingly — a series of iron anchors that match anchors from vessels not yet built at the time of their estimated deposition. One anchor bears the registry number of a ship still operating. The examination concluded without a published finding.

ecology_01.title: Plants Found Nowhere Else
ecology_01.body: Six flowering species on the island have no identified relatives in any botanical survey of the surrounding region. They are not exotic imports — their root structures show thousands of years of soil adaptation. They arrived, or appeared, before the first human settlers. One species flowers only when the lighthouse beam fails to complete its rotation.

ecology_02.title: What Makes the Water Glow
ecology_02.body: The bioluminescence in the tidal caves and the surrounding shallows does not match any known marine organism. Under analysis, the light-producing compound corresponds structurally to the phosphor used in the lighthouse lens — which was not synthesised until three centuries after the caves' first recorded exploration. The relationship between the two sources has not been explained.

ecology_03.title: Animals That Know to Avoid
ecology_03.body: No bird nests in the lighthouse tower or on the ruins. No dog will cross the threshold of the mechanism room. The island's animals do not appear to be afraid — they simply route around certain spaces as if those spaces do not exist. Cats are the exception. They enter everywhere. They do not appear disturbed by what they find.

ecology_04.title: A Path That Lengthens
ecology_04.body: The forest path from the village edge to the mill is, by ground survey, approximately four hundred metres. It consistently takes longer to walk than four hundred metres at normal pace. The discrepancy is not constant — it is greater on nights when the mechanism is active. The path does not fork. The additional distance goes somewhere.

ecology_05.title: One Root, One Forest
ecology_05.body: Core samples from trees across the island reveal a shared root architecture. Every tree is, in biological terms, a single organism — distinct trunks sharing one continuous root network that extends throughout the island's soil. The network is old enough that separating its age from the island's age is not possible with current methods. The roots pass under the lighthouse foundation.

ecology_06.title: A Cliff That Does Not Erode
ecology_06.body: The northern cliff face shows no measurable change in the survey records spanning sixty years. Coastal erosion at comparable limestone formations produces an average of twelve centimetres of retreat per decade. Something is stabilising the cliff. The surveys note anomalous mineral deposits along the face — compounds not associated with the local geology, appearing at regular intervals.

ecology_07.title: The Moving Dead Zone
ecology_07.body: A dead zone in the water near the lighthouse cliff — an area where marine life is absent — has been documented for decades. It is not constant. It moves, approximately, in correspondence with the reported position of the shape beneath the water. The dead zone is roughly the shape of a large body in repose. Nothing grows where it has recently been.

ecology_08.title: Why the Stream Stopped
ecology_08.body: The mill's stream ceased flowing approximately twenty years ago. The water table upstream shows no depletion. The channel simply stopped carrying water, at a point corresponding with the stream's passage under the ruins' eastern wall. Excavation of the channel at that point was begun and abandoned. The workers declined to say what they found. The channel remains sealed.

ecology_09.title: What Grows in the Mill's Shadow
ecology_09.body: The grain cultivated on fields north of the mill shows consistent morphological differences from the same variety grown elsewhere on the island — broader seed casings, lower germination rates, an oil content that does not match the parent crop's profile. The fields have been growing this strain for as long as anyone records. No one brought it here. It arrived with the shadow.

ecology_10.title: The Island's Contradictory Age
ecology_10.body: Carbon dating of the oldest visible rock formation gives a result consistent with regional geology: several million years. Dating of material from the lowest archive basement stratum gives a result of three hundred years, which is impossible given the stratum's depth. Dating of the mechanism room's stone gives a result older than the island. The results are not reconcilable.

alchemy_01.title: Symbols in the Mechanism Room
alchemy_01.body: The symbols carved into the mechanism room walls are not decorative and not merely linguistic. They are operational instructions — each glyph specifying a quantity, a temperature, a duration, or a compound. Followed in sequence, they describe a process that does not correspond to any known alchemical tradition, but whose outputs are recognisable: the phosphor compound, the binding paste, and a third product with no documented application.

alchemy_02.title: Why the Beam Burns Differently
alchemy_02.body: The lighthouse lens burns a phosphor compound that is not commercially available and cannot be reproduced from any published formula. Analysis of the residue indicates seventeen components, four of which are not identifiable by current methods. The compound burns without heat. It does not consume itself at the rate a combustion reaction would predict. The supply in the lamp room has not noticeably diminished in three recorded visits.

alchemy_03.title: Substances Used in the Original Seal
alchemy_03.body: The binding seal that holds the Vael is not purely symbolic. A physical compound was laid into the lighthouse foundation at its construction — visible as a dark mineral seam in the basement stone. The compound contains iron, salt, and a third component that resists standard analysis. When the seal is disturbed, the compound produces a low vibration felt in the lower bones. It does not emit light. It absorbs it.

alchemy_04.title: The Parts That Aren't There
alchemy_04.body: The mechanism room's inventory, when catalogued, produces a count of components that does not correspond to the mechanism's observable function. There are parts that serve no mechanical purpose. There are also gaps — spaces where components should be, by the logic of the machine's operation, but are not. Some keepers have reported seeing the machine complete its rotation without the missing parts. Others report the gaps are sometimes filled.

alchemy_05.title: Keepers Age Differently
alchemy_05.body: Portraits of successive keepers, compared at the end of their respective years, show inconsistent aging. Some show no change. Some show changes equivalent to a decade. At least two show apparent physical regression. The variable does not correlate with workload or health records. It correlates, imperfectly, with how closely each keeper approached the mechanism's lower chamber.

alchemy_06.title: What the Vael Leaves Behind
alchemy_06.body: In locations where the entity's presence has been documented — the tidal caves, the mechanism room's lower chamber, the space beneath the lighthouse foundation — a residue accumulates. It is dark, has no odour, and absorbs light in a manner that makes precise examination difficult. Samples removed from the site undergo rapid deterioration. One researcher described the residue as 'time made material.' The description has not been improved upon.

alchemy_07.title: Ink That Persists Unnaturally
alchemy_07.body: Certain documents in the archive are written in a substance that behaves differently from organic ink. It does not fade. It does not absorb into the paper — it sits atop the fibres as if the paper were unwilling to accept it. Attempts to destroy these documents have been unsuccessful. The ink, when spectrally analysed, contains no carbon. It is not ink. What it is has not been determined.

alchemy_08.title: What Purification Does Not Remove
alchemy_08.body: The cleansing ritual documented in the keeper manuals — salt water, specific chant, threshold crossing at dawn — removes certain categories of contamination. It does not remove Vael residue. It does not remove the temporal distortion associated with loop proximity. Several keepers performed it daily as a matter of comfort. Its practical effect appears to be primarily psychological, though this may underestimate its actual function.

alchemy_09.title: Why the Well Was Sealed
alchemy_09.body: The village well was sealed not because it ran dry but because of what was found at its base: a deposit of the same binding compound used in the lighthouse foundation, at a depth that precedes the well's construction. Someone placed it there before the well was dug. The compound was intact. Its position places it at the intersection of several of the island's threshold stone circuits.

alchemy_10.title: What the Mechanism Takes
alchemy_10.body: Each operation of the mechanism extracts something from the keeper who performs it. The extraction is not painful and not immediately apparent. It resembles, in aggregate, the specific quality of certainty — the conviction that one's memories are accurate and that the past is fixed. Keepers who have served multiple years describe a progressive difficulty distinguishing what they have experienced from what they have imagined. This is not considered a side effect. It is considered the cost.

cartography_01.title: Absent from All Charts
cartography_01.body: The island appears on fewer than a dozen maps in the known cartographic record, and in each case the notation suggests the cartographer was uncertain whether to include it. Most charts of the surrounding region show empty water at its coordinates. The island is not hidden by classification or error. It appears to choose, in some manner not yet explained, when to be visible to the instruments of mapping.

cartography_02.title: What the First Survey Found
cartography_02.body: The first formal land survey of the island, conducted approximately one hundred and forty years after settlement, records a geography that does not fully match the current topography. Several features noted by the surveyor cannot be located today. Several features that exist today are not mentioned. The surveyor's accompanying notes describe an island that seemed, during the three-week survey, to be 'adjusting to observation.'

cartography_03.title: A Network Below the Paths
cartography_03.body: Evidence from multiple sources — keeper logs, cave exploration records, the behaviour of groundwater — indicates an extensive network of passages beneath the island's visible surface. The network connects the lighthouse foundation to the tidal caves, and possibly to the mill's original drainage system. Only one passage has been entered and returned from. The explorer's account describes it as 'navigating the island's memory.'

cartography_04.title: Not Where Charts Say It Is
cartography_04.body: When the lighthouse's position is calculated from its observed bearing from known coastal landmarks, the result places it approximately two hundred metres from where all internal maps locate it. The discrepancy is consistent across different calculation methods. Either every landmark has been measured incorrectly, or the lighthouse is not entirely fixed to its visible location. Both possibilities have been considered. Neither has been dismissed.

cartography_05.title: Caves Deeper Than Anyone Has Mapped
cartography_05.body: The tidal caves' full extent has never been mapped. Expeditions have reached a depth where the cave walls begin to exhibit the same regular proportions as the upper chambers, suggesting the artificial shaping continues. Beyond that point, navigation becomes unreliable — compasses produce inconsistent readings, and the sense of direction required to return to the entrance is impaired in a way that does not respond to practice or familiarity.

cartography_06.title: Structures on the Seabed
cartography_06.body: A survey of the seabed around the island, compiled from diver records over several decades, reveals a pattern of foundations beneath the water that mirrors the island's surface buildings — but is not aligned with them. Each surface building has a correspondent below. The correspondences are not coincidental: they are at identical bearings from the lighthouse. Whatever is below was the template for what is above.

cartography_07.title: The Island Rearranges Itself
cartography_07.body: The island's geography is not static. Path widths, building positions, the cliff's contour — all shift, slowly, across the span of years that multiple records cover. The changes are too small to perceive in a single visit and too consistent to attribute to error. The lighthouse is the one fixed point. Everything else drifts, incrementally, in patterns that suggest not random movement but deliberate adjustment.

cartography_08.title: The Mill Has Moved
cartography_08.body: The mill's position in the earliest cadastral record differs from its current position by approximately forty metres. The original foundation can still be found beneath the undergrowth behind the current building — the same stone, the same millstone anchor points, the same drainage channel orientation. The mill was lifted and moved, at some point, without any record of when or why. The original position was directly above the underground passage that has never been fully mapped.

cartography_09.title: A Much Larger Original Building
cartography_09.body: The ruins' visible extent accounts for approximately one-fifth of the original structure's footprint, extrapolated from the surviving foundation lines. The full building would have been the island's largest by a significant margin — larger than the lighthouse, larger than the archive. Its eastern wing's door remains standing, in the current ruins, attached to nothing. The door's frame is carved with the names of the three previous keepers.

cartography_10.title: Where the Island Ends
cartography_10.body: There is a point, beyond the cliff's farthest extent, where the island ends — not geographically but in some other sense. Keepers have described it as a place where the light from the lighthouse does not reach, though this is geometrically impossible. Beyond this boundary, the loop's rules appear to apply differently. One keeper recorded standing beyond it and watching, from outside, the island's light go dark and return. She did not describe what happened in the interval.

linguistics_01.title: What the Island Was Called
linguistics_01.body: The island's original name, in the notation system predating settlement, translates most closely as 'the listening place.' The translation is disputed — a minority interpretation renders it as 'the place that listens.' The distinction matters: one is a description of function, the other an attribution of agency. Both translations use the same root for 'listen.' The difference is in the grammatical role assigned to the island.

linguistics_02.title: Every Keeper Learned This System
linguistics_02.body: Each keeper, regardless of their prior education or background, adopted a consistent private notation system within their first season. The system is not taught. No manual for it has ever been found. Yet the notation in keeper journals from across three centuries is mutually legible — the same symbols, the same conventions, the same specific use of margin marks. Where they learned it is not documented. It may not have been a learning process.

linguistics_03.title: The Chapel Inscription Is a Warning
linguistics_03.body: The chapel's foundation inscription, carved into the stone below the main door's threshold, has been read by most visitors as a dedication. It is not. Translated from the pre-settlement notation, it reads: 'What is held here was not made here. What was made here cannot leave. Enter only if you understand the difference.' The word for 'understand,' in this usage, implies physical rather than intellectual comprehension.

linguistics_04.title: Thirty-Seven Symbols, No Known Source
linguistics_04.body: The rune system found on the island's threshold stones, tidal cave walls, and mechanism room surfaces comprises thirty-seven distinct symbols. It does not correspond to any documented script. Structural analysis suggests it was designed as a closed system — it contains no mechanism for adding new symbols, because its inventors believed the thirty-seven covered every concept that would ever require notation here. Seventeen of those concepts have no equivalent in any known language.

linguistics_05.title: What the Founding Family Names Meant
linguistics_05.body: The families present at the island's founding bore names that, in the pre-settlement notation, each describe a function rather than an identity: one translates as 'the one who maintains,' another as 'the one who observes,' a third as 'the one who records what cannot be recorded.' The names were not inherited from earlier generations. They were adopted at the moment of the island's founding. The families chose their roles before they understood what the roles required.

linguistics_06.title: What the Cave Walls Say
linguistics_06.body: Of the tidal text's deciphered sections, three-quarters describe what appears to be a binding negotiation — terms offered, modified, refused, and finally agreed to. The entity referred to throughout is not named. The human parties are identified by function rather than name. The final agreed term, in the legible portion of the text, is partial: '...and in exchange, the loop shall serve as—' The rest is submerged. It has not yet been read.

linguistics_07.title: What the Tree Symbols Mean
linguistics_07.body: The symbols carved into trees along the forest path are navigational — but not in any spatial sense. They mark points of temporal significance: places where a previous loop diverged, where a keeper made a choice that changed the pattern. Read in sequence, they constitute a partial map of what has already been attempted. Several symbols indicate outcomes. Most of the outcome symbols are the same.

linguistics_08.title: A Language Before Language
linguistics_08.body: The fragments of Vael's direct communication that have been recorded — in keeper journals, in the tidal text, in notation found carved into the mechanism room's oldest stones — do not form a language in any conventional sense. They form a set of impressions that acquire meaning through the context of the reader's own experience. Two keepers who read the same fragment reported entirely different translations, both internally consistent. The Vael speaks in the space between what you know and what you fear.

linguistics_09.title: A Second Hand in the Ledger
linguistics_09.body: The supply ledger contains two layers of annotation — the official record in one hand, and a second, smaller notation in the margins and between lines. The second notation is in keeper code, distinct from the standard private notation in its use of a substitution cipher. Decoded, the marginal notes describe not supplies but conditions: 'third night, entity restless.' 'Seventh night, beam failed three seconds, sequence disrupted.' 'Month four, I no longer hear it as sound.'

linguistics_10.title: The Mechanism Tells You What to Do
linguistics_10.body: The inscription running continuously around the mechanism room's upper wall is not descriptive — it is instructional. Each of its forty-four symbols corresponds to a specific physical action the keeper must perform, in sequence, to correctly operate the mechanism. The inscription has been there since the mechanism was built. Every keeper has followed its instructions. None of them, until now, appear to have known they were reading it.

old_journal.label: Old Journal
old_journal.text: The journal belongs to the previous keeper. Most entries are methodical — weather, mechanism checks, supply counts — until the final month, when the handwriting contracts and the entries become fragmentary. The last page reads: 'It is not the mechanism that needs tending. It never was. I understand that now, and understanding it has cost me something I cannot name.' The page after it has been torn out.

mechanism_diagram.label: Mechanism Diagram
mechanism_diagram.text: A hand-drawn diagram of the lighthouse mechanism, annotated in two different hands. The original draughtsman drew gears, shafts, and tolerances. A second hand has overlaid the drawing with a completely different notation — not mechanical but symbolic, each component labelled with a rune rather than a measurement. The two systems do not contradict each other. They describe the same object in two entirely different languages.

lens_inscription.label: Lens Inscription
lens_inscription.text: Etched into the brass housing of the lens, following the curve of the frame: a line of text in the island's pre-settlement notation. Your translation is slow and uncertain, but the central phrase resolves clearly enough — 'to hold, not to guide.' The lens does not illuminate the sea. It illuminates the thing beneath it. The distinction matters.

keepers_last_log.label: Keeper's Last Log
keepers_last_log.text: The final entry in the log mounted beside the lens is dated to the night the previous keeper disappeared. The handwriting is steady — steadier than the journal entries below suggest it should be. 'The mechanism is maintained. The seal holds. I am leaving this post not because I have failed but because I have understood what success requires, and I have decided I am not willing to pay it again. Whoever reads this: the interval between the second and third pulse is not mechanical. Do not let it lengthen.'

sealed_ledger.label: Sealed Ledger
sealed_ledger.text: The ledger is sealed with wax bearing an impression you don't recognise — not a family crest, not an official mark, something older. When you break it open, the first page is a list of names, each with a date and a single word alongside it. The word changes: 'departed,' 'expired,' 'absorbed.' The most recent entry is three names from the bottom. You recognise the name. You have spoken to this person. The date is seven years ago.

water_line_mark.label: Water Line Mark
water_line_mark.text: A dark stain on the archive wall, precisely horizontal, approximately one metre above the floor. The flood that left it was not recent — the stain is dry and old, the stone behind it faintly salt-crusted. What unsettles you is its height relative to the shelving. The documents on the lower shelves show no water damage. Either the flood didn't reach them, or they were moved here afterwards, from somewhere the flood did reach.

manifest_discrepancy.label: Manifest Discrepancy
manifest_discrepancy.text: The cargo manifest for the last supply ship lists forty-three items. The harbour's receiving ledger, which should mirror it, lists thirty-nine. The four missing items are not crossed out or marked as refused — they simply do not appear. The items in question are listed on the manifest as 'compound, sealed, four canisters.' Silas signed both documents. His signature on the receiving ledger is in a different ink.

silas_logbook.label: Silas's Logbook
silas_logbook.text: Silas's personal logbook is not the official harbour record — this is a second book, kept separately, with entries that do not correspond to any official arrival or departure. The entries describe ships you find no record of elsewhere, with cargo described in terms that are not commercial. One entry, repeated across seven different dates, reads only: 'delivered as agreed.' You cannot find what was delivered, or what was agreed, or with whom.

mechanism_symbols.label: Mechanism Symbols
mechanism_symbols.text: You spend time copying the symbols from the walls into your notebook, working around the room in a methodical circuit. It is only when you step back and read your own transcription that you understand: you have been reading them in the wrong order. The circuit runs counterclockwise. Read correctly, the symbols are not a description of the mechanism's function — they are instructions for its use, step by step, beginning with a phrase that translates as 'before you touch it, confirm your intention.'

binding_rune.label: Binding Rune
binding_rune.text: The central rune on the mechanism room's floor is larger than the others and set slightly into the stone rather than carved onto its surface, as if pressed there under pressure from above. When you place your hand near it — not on it, near it — the air changes. Not temperature. Something else. The rune's translation, which you work out slowly, is a verb in a tense that does not exist in any language you know: an action that is simultaneously completed, ongoing, and not yet begun.

burned_portrait.label: Burned Portrait
burned_portrait.text: The portrait has been burned from the edges inward, deliberately — whoever did this wanted the face preserved longest. The face is almost intact: a keeper, by their dress, looking directly at the painter with an expression that is not quite calm and not quite afraid. The nameplate beneath the frame has been removed. On the back of the canvas, in handwriting you are beginning to recognise as the second-hand annotation style from the supply ledger, someone has written a single word: 'complicit.'

east_wing_door.label: East Wing Door
east_wing_door.text: The door stands in open air, its frame intact, the stonework that once surrounded it entirely gone. It opens — you try the handle, and it opens — onto the same open air on the other side. You note the three names carved into the frame: the three previous keepers, each name rendered in the keeper's private notation rather than standard letters. You recognise the notation now. The names are not just names. They are, in keeper notation, three different words for 'threshold.'

hidden_names.label: Hidden Names
hidden_names.text: Behind the altar, at eye level when kneeling, a section of plaster has been painted over several times. Enough layers have peeled that the original surface is partially visible. Names, written in charcoal directly onto the stone: dozens of them, covering the entire surface behind the altar. Some you recognise from the archive records. Some are in the keeper notation. At the bottom, added in a different hand and more recently, is your own name. The charcoal is not dry.

cracked_saint.label: Cracked Saint
cracked_saint.text: The crack in the stained glass runs directly through the figure's face, splitting it from crown to chin. Through the gap, the rough plaster of the repair is visible — a botched job, the filler not colour-matched, applied in haste. What draws your attention is the fragment of the original face that the crack has displaced: a sliver of glass, still held in the lead, showing one eye. The eye is not looking outward, as the figure's posture suggests. It is looking down, into the pit beneath the flagstone floor.

shape_in_water.label: Shape in the Water
shape_in_water.text: From this height, the shape is clearer than from below. It is not moving, currently — it occupies the same position it has held through your last three visits, a dark displacement in the water that catches the light wrong. Its outline is not fixed: the edges shift, contract, extend, in a rhythm you eventually identify as matching the lighthouse beam's rotation. When the beam passes over it, the shape stills. In the interval between passes, it breathes.

rope_marks_on_rock.label: Rope Marks on Rock
rope_marks_on_rock.text: The marks are deep, worn into the cliff edge by repeated use over many years — not a single descent but dozens, perhaps hundreds. The most recent marks are recent enough that the stone is still pale where the rock has been abraded. Someone has been going down to the water regularly, with equipment, in a way they did not want noted in any harbour record. The marks are on the side of the cliff directly above the shape.

bioluminescent_symbols.label: Bioluminescent Symbols
bioluminescent_symbols.text: The symbols glow faintly and steadily, unaffected by your lantern. Close examination reveals they were not painted but cultivated — the bioluminescent organism has been shaped into the symbol forms, encouraged to grow in specific patterns over what must have been years. Whoever made these was not writing a message in the ordinary sense. They were growing one, slowly, in a medium that would last as long as the tide kept coming.

waterlogged_chest.label: Waterlogged Chest
waterlogged_chest.text: The chest's lock gave way to salt and time before you arrived. Inside: a keeper's uniform, folded carefully; a compass whose needle points consistently toward the lighthouse foundation rather than magnetic north; and a small notebook whose pages have fused but whose cover is legible. The keeper's name on the cover is one of the three carved into the east wing door. The date is sixty years before anyone currently living arrived on this island.

disturbed_earth.label: Disturbed Earth
disturbed_earth.text: The ground here has been recently turned — within the last few days, you estimate, given the moisture retention at the surface. The disturbance is roughly rectangular, approximately the size of a large case or a small chest. Whatever was here has been removed, not buried. The soil at the bottom of the impression carries the same faint mineral smell as the mechanism room's lower chamber. Something was stored here and has recently been moved.

carved_tree.label: Carved Tree
carved_tree.text: The carvings on this tree are different from the navigational marks on the others — more complex, layered, as if added to over many visits. At the base, the oldest inscription is almost illegible with growth. Near eye level, the most recent addition is clear: a date, rendered in keeper notation, followed by a symbol you have seen once before, on the sealed ledger. The symbol means, approximately, 'point of no return.' The date is two weeks ago.

dry_well.label: Dry Well
dry_well.text: The planks sealing the well are nailed from above, but the nails are not recent — they are corroded, the wood around them stained with rust, the job done years ago. You pry one board up enough to see down. The well is not empty in the usual sense. Something occupies it — not water, not debris. A darkness that is slightly more present than ordinary darkness, as if the space has been filled with something that absorbs rather than reflects. You replace the board carefully.

notice_board.label: Notice Board
notice_board.text: The notice board carries the ordinary life of the village — a lost boat to be returned, a surplus of salted fish, a request for a specific repair tool. Behind these, partially obscured, is an older notice, its paper brittle. It is a reminder of the island's annual observance: 'The keeper's year ends at the autumn tide. All residents are reminded to remain indoors from dusk until the light confirms.' The notice does not specify what the light is confirming, or what happens if it does not.

millers_last_log.label: Miller's Last Log
millers_last_log.text: The log's final entry is not an ending — it stops mid-sentence, the pen having apparently been set down and not returned to. 'The water has been still for three weeks now but the wheel turns regardless and I have stopped pretending I know what is turning it. Last night I heard the mechanism from here, which is impossible given the distance, but I heard it, and then I heard something answer it from below the—' The sentence does not continue. The pen is still on the table.

hidden_trapdoor.label: Hidden Trapdoor
hidden_trapdoor.text: The trapdoor is concealed under the grinding stone's housing, accessible only when the stone is raised. Below it: stone steps descending into a passage that runs, by your reckoning, in the direction of the lighthouse. The passage smells of the same mineral compound as the archive basement and the mechanism room. On the top step, scratched into the stone as if in haste, is a keeper notation symbol you now know well. It translates as 'below is older than above.'
